preFix.title: Replacing a toilet seat
preFix.subtitle: Remove a worn or broken toilet seat and fit a replacement with the correct fixings
preFix.excerpt: How to remove an old toilet seat from a standard close-coupled pan and fit a new seat, covering undoing the hinge bolts, cleaning the fixing pads, and fitting the new hinges with the correct top-fixing or bottom-fixing method.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Plumbing for Beginners (1918). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Toilet seats are fixed to the pan by a pair of hinge bolts through fixing holes at the back of the rim. Most modern seats use
that unclip for cleaning without undoing the bolts. The main measurement for a replacement seat is the fixing hole centres: standard UK close-coupled pans take a 155 mm fixing centre.

A replacement seat and lid to match the pan shape (round or D-shaped, standard or comfort height). Measure the pan length and width and the hinge fixing-hole centres before buying. Soft-close mechanisms add bulk but reduce noise. The new seat will come with its hinge fixing kit. Old seats may be fixed with brass bolts and nuts that have corroded solid: have the adjustable spanner and pliers ready.

Open the plastic cover caps over the hinge bolt heads on the top of the seat and press them back. On a top-fixing seat, hold the bolt head from above with a screwdriver and undo the wing nut below the rim by hand or with the pliers. On an older seat with a bottom bolt, hold the nut below the rim and undo the bolt from above. Corroded steel bolts that will not undo by hand can be cut with a junior hacksaw slid under the rim. Lift the old seat off the pan.

Clean the area around the fixing holes on the pan rim. Old rubber fixing pads often leave marks on the glaze: a nylon scouring pad removes them without scratching the pan. Check that the fixing holes in the pan rim are not cracked around the edges: a cracked fixing hole needs a larger rubber pad under the new hinge to spread the load. Dry the surface before fitting the new seat.

Feed the hinge bolts down through the seat hinge holes and then down through the pan fixing holes. Below the rim, slide the rubber buffers and plastic washers onto the bolt ends, then thread on the wing nuts. Hand-tighten the wing nuts until the rubber buffers press against the underside of the rim and the seat does not rock. Give each wing nut a further quarter-turn with the pliers. Overtightening can crack a ceramic pan rim: stop as soon as the seat is firm.
Step 4, check alignment and snap on the cover caps

Sit the seat in the closed position and check that it is centred over the pan opening with equal margins at each side. If the seat is not centred, loosen the wing nuts and slide the seat across the fixing holes before re-tightening. Most fixing bolt slots allow 5 to 10 mm of side adjustment. Press the decorative caps back over the bolt heads until they click into place.

A seat with a quick-release hinge has a two-part fixing: a wall plate bolts to the pan and the seat clicks onto the wall plate. Fitting follows the same bolt method; the seat then clips into place. For a wall-hung WC, the seat is fixed the same way but the cistern is concealed and the rim shape differs from a floor-standing pan. Check the model number of the WC before buying a replacement seat.

Check the hinge wing nuts every six months: they loosen with use. Hand-tighten if the seat rocks. Clean around the hinge fixing area regularly; moisture trapped under the rubber pads causes mould growth. A soft-close mechanism that closes too fast can be adjusted by a small screw on the hinge pivot.

attrs.fix: Tighten the wing nuts until the buffers hold the seat firm, then give a quarter-turn more with the pliers. If the holes in the pan are larger than the bolt, fit a larger rubber pad or washer to fill the gap.
attrs.cause: The wing nuts are not tight enough, or the rubber buffers have compressed and the bolts have room to move in the pan fixing holes.

attrs.fix: Spray the nut with penetrating oil and leave for 15 minutes. If still stuck, slide a junior hacksaw blade under the rim and saw through the bolt shank. The old seat lifts off; the cut stump can be wiggled out from above.
attrs.cause: Steel hinge bolts corrode against the damp ceramic, fusing the thread to the nut.
attrs.symptom: Old hinge bolts will not undo: seized solid
